title: Agropyron peruvianum
intro: Agropyron peruvianum, also known as Quenual grass or Queñua grass, is a plant species native to the Andean regions of South America, characterized by its dense, feathery foliage and long, narrow spikelets. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, inflammation, and respiratory problems, thanks to its reported anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Agropyron peruvianum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are believed to contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared as a decoction or infused in hot water to make a tea, and it is also used in the form of a tincture or as a powder for the treatment of various health conditions.
taxonomy: Agropyron peruvianum is classified as a species of grass within the family Poaceae. It is a type of wild grass native to certain regions. More specifically, Agropyron peruvianum is classified in the genus Agropyron, which is a group of wild grasses. The taxonomy of Agropyron peruvianum is further detailed as Agropyron peruvianum is in the tribe Triticeae, and then in the order Poales. The exact classification is Agropyron peruvianum (Trin.) Parodi, but note that classifications can change over time.
